title: Frank Connor and Trooper Werner Foerster Justice Act
short_title: Frank Connor and Trooper Werner Foerster Justice Act
plain_summary: This bill wants Cuba to send back criminals who ran away to Cuba to hide from the police in America. These criminals did very bad things like terrorism and murder in the United States. The bill says the government must write reports every year about trying to get these criminals back. It also says America will not give certain money to Cuba until Cuba sends the criminals back to face punishment in American courts.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Frank Connor and Trooper Werner Foerster Justice Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Findings
sections.text: Congress has found the following facts. Joanne Chesimard is on the FBI's list of Most Wanted Terrorists and is believed to be hiding in Cuba to avoid prison for crimes she committed in the United States. On May second nineteen seventy three, Ms. Chesimard was a member of the Black Liberation Army extremist organization. She and two other people shot at two New Jersey State troopers during a traffic stop. Ms. Chesimard and her accomplices wounded one State trooper and killed State Trooper Werner Foerster by shooting him at close range. After a six week trial in March nineteen seventy seven, Ms. Chesimard was found guilty of first degree murder and sentenced to life in prison. On November second nineteen seventy nine, Ms. Chesimard escaped from what is now the Edna Mahan Correctional Facility for Women with help from armed people pretending to be visitors. She then fled to Cuba. William Guillermo Morales was a bomb maker for the terrorist organization Fuerzas Armadas de Liberacion Nacional. He is believed to have committed many terrorist attacks on United States soil. This includes the bombing of Fraunces Tavern in lower Manhattan on January twenty fifth nineteen seventy five and the bombing of the Mobil Oil employment office in New York on August third nineteen seventy seven. Among those killed in the Fraunces Tavern bombing was Mr. Frank Connor of New Jersey. In July nineteen seventy eight, William Guillermo Morales was hospitalized at Bellevue Hospital after a bomb he was making exploded too early. He then escaped to Mexico and made his way to Cuba before June nineteen eighty eight. Other fugitives from the United States who have been charged with crimes like hijacking, kidnapping, drug trafficking, and murder are believed to be hiding safely in Cuba. Fugitives from the United States who are currently living in Cuba include Charlie Hill, a member of the Republic of New Afrika militant group who is accused of killing a policeman in New Mexico in nineteen seventy one before hijacking a passenger plane and getting asylum in Cuba. Also Victor Manuel Gerena, a member of the Puerto Rican terrorist group Los Macheteros who stole a Wells Fargo armored car in Connecticut containing over seven million dollars in November nineteen eighty three before escaping to Cuba and remained on the FBI's Ten Most Wanted Fugitives list for more than thirty two years. The Treaty Between the United States and Cuba for the Mutual Extradition of Fugitives from Justice was signed in Washington DC on April sixth nineteen oh four. The Additional Extradition Treaty Between the United States and Cuba was signed in Havana Cuba on January fourteenth nineteen twenty six. These are bilateral extradition treaties between the United States and Cuba (treaties where both countries agree to send fugitives back to each other). The Cuban government has previously returned fugitives from the United States. This includes Jesse James Bell, a United States citizen wanted on fifteen drug charges who was returned to the United States in January two thousand two. Leonard B. Auerbach, a United States citizen wanted on Federal child sex crimes charges who was returned to the United States in June two thousand eight. And James Ray the third, a United States citizen and New Jersey resident accused of murdering his girlfriend, who was returned to the United States in November two thousand eighteen.

sections.title: Section 3 Sense of Congress
sections.text: Congress believes the following. Joanne Chesimard, William Guillermo Morales, and all other fugitives hiding safely in Cuba to escape prosecution or prison for criminal offenses committed in the United States must be extradited or returned immediately to the United States (extradited means sent back to face trial or serve their sentence). This should happen according to the Cuban government's duties under its extradition treaties with the United States. The Secretary of State and the Attorney General should use all appropriate diplomatic and policy tools to secure the timely extradition or return of all fugitives living in Cuba to face justice in the United States.

sections.title: Section 4 Annual Report and Determination on Fugitives from the United States in Cuba
sections.text: The Secretary of State, working with the Attorney General, shall bring up the issue of fugitives from the United States hiding safely in Cuba as part of bilateral conversations with the Cuban government (bilateral conversations means talks between the two countries). Not later than one hundred eighty days after this law is enacted, and every year after that until the date specified in part c of this section, the Secretary of State shall submit a report to the Committee on Foreign Relations of the Senate and the Committee on Foreign Affairs of the House of Representatives. The report must identify steps taken by the Department of State to advance efforts to secure the extradition or return of Joanne Chesimard, William Guillermo Morales, and other fugitives from the United States who are living in Cuba. The report must include a determination as to whether the Cuban government is actively fulfilling its obligations under the bilateral extradition treaties between the United States and Cuba described in section two part six. To the extent feasible, the report must include an estimate of the number of fugitives from the United States who are hiding safely in Cuba. The Secretary of State is not required to submit the report described in part b after the date on which the Secretary submits a second consecutive annual report under such part that includes a determination that the Cuban government is actively fulfilling its extradition obligations and is returning fugitives of the United States who are living in Cuba.

sections.title: Section 5 Prohibition on the Use of INCLE Funding in Cuba
sections.text: Money deposited into the International Narcotics Control and Law Enforcement account to carry out the activities authorized under section four hundred eighty one part a part four of the Foreign Assistance Act of nineteen sixty one may not be used for programs or initiatives in Cuba until the Cuban government is in compliance with the conditions set forth in paragraphs one and two of section four part c of this Act and the conditions set for the resumption of economic activity between the United States and Cuba pursuant to law, including the Cuban Liberty and Democratic Solidarity LIBERTAD Act of nineteen ninety six.
